subsystem: crun container runtime — the `crun exec` command handler (crun_command_exec). crun is a fast OCI runtime written in C. This function parses the command line for `crun exec`, assembles an OCI process spec (runtime_spec_schema_config_schema_process) for the process to be launched inside an already-running container, and hands it to libcrun_container_exec_with_options to actually enter the container namespaces and exec the target program.
provenance: Identified from the sample banner `/* sample: primevul_c_test_0136_vuln */` and the crun-specific symbols: crun_global_arguments, libcrun_context_t, libcrun_error_t, cleanup_process_schema, runtime_spec_schema_config_schema_process, xmalloc0/xstrdup/dup_array, make_oci_process_user, libcrun_container_exec_with_options, and the argp-based CLI (run_argp, argp_parse, crun_assert_n_args). These are all names from containers/crun (src/crun.c / src/exec.c and libcrun). The sample stem `cwe276` points at CWE-276 Incorrect Default Permissions.
trust_boundary: This is a privilege-boundary tool, not a wire parser. Inputs are the argv/argc passed to `crun exec` and the process environment (LISTEN_FDS). The security-relevant boundary is the privilege of the process that gets exec'd INTO the container: crun runs with elevated privileges and must ensure the new process is confined at least as tightly as the container was created with. The hostile surface is any default that is silently LOOSER than the container's original config — a missing no_new_privileges bit or unset capability set means the exec'd process is more privileged than intended (privilege escalation), regardless of who supplies the args. LISTEN_FDS is attacker/environment-controlled text.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point for `crun exec`. It receives the parsed global arguments, the raw argc/argv for the subcommand, and an out-parameter err for error reporting. first_arg will hold the index in argv where positional args begin (filled by argp). crun_context is a zero-initialized libcrun_context_t that carries per-invocation runtime settings. process is a heap pointer to an OCI process schema, declared with the cleanup_process_schema attribute so it is auto-freed on scope exit; it is used only when the user did NOT pass a pre-built process spec. exec_opts is the options struct handed to the exec engine.

exec_opts is zeroed and then its struct_size field is stamped with sizeof(exec_opts). This is a versioning/ABI guard so the callee libcrun_container_exec_with_options can tell which fields the caller knows about. Because the whole struct is memset to zero first, every option field defaults to 0/NULL unless explicitly assigned later — that default-zero behavior is exactly what makes the missing no_new_privileges / capabilities assignments below dangerous.

Initializes the file-descriptor pass-through counters. preserve_fds is the number of extra inherited FDs to keep open across the exec; listen_fds is the socket-activation FD count. Both start at zero and are later overridden from CLI options and the LISTEN_FDS environment variable.

what_the_code_does.code:
argp_parse (&run_argp, argc, argv, ARGP_IN_ORDER, &first_arg, &exec_options);
  crun_assert_n_args (argc - first_arg, exec_options.process ? 1 : 2, -1);
what_the_code_does.explanation: argp_parse walks argv against the run_argp option table, populating the file-scope exec_options structure and setting first_arg to the index of the first non-option argument. ARGP_IN_ORDER preserves argument order. crun_assert_n_args then validates the count of positional args: if the user passed a `--process` spec file (exec_options.process set) exactly 1 positional arg is required (the container id), otherwise 2 (container id plus the command). This is the only arity check before the args are consumed below.

init_libcrun_context finishes filling crun_context, keyed by the container id argv[first_arg] (the first positional argument), merging in the global arguments. On any negative return it propagates the error immediately. After crun_assert_n_args this index is known to be in range.

Copies plain CLI-supplied options into the runtime context: whether to detach, the console socket path for terminal forwarding, the pid file to write, and the preserve_fds count. These are direct pass-throughs of user intent and carry no size/length contracts.

Implements systemd socket-activation semantics: if the LISTEN_FDS environment variable is present, its decimal value is parsed with strtoll and stored as listen_fds, then ADDED to preserve_fds so those activation sockets are inherited by the container process. The value is taken directly from the environment with no range or error checking.

If the user supplied `--process <file>`, crun takes the OCI process spec from that file: it just records the path in exec_opts.path and skips building a process struct entirely. In this branch none of the capability / no_new_privileges defaulting below runs — the spec file is trusted to carry those settings.

Otherwise crun synthesizes a process spec from the command line. It zero-allocates the process struct, records args_len = argc, allocates an argv array of (argc+1) char* slots, and copies the command tokens. The loop runs argc - first_arg times, copying argv[first_arg + i + 1] (i.e. the args AFTER the container id) via xstrdup, then NUL-terminates the array at index i. Note that args_len is set to argc but only argc - first_arg + (the terminator) entries are actually meaningful — see the risks.

Fills the remaining process fields from CLI options: working directory (only if provided), terminal/tty flag, the environment array with its length env_size, and the uid/gid struct built by make_oci_process_user from the `--user` string. These are straightforward option copies establishing the runtime identity and environment of the exec'd process.

Conditionally sets the SELinux process label and the AppArmor profile, but only when the user explicitly passed them. If the flags are absent the fields stay NULL (from xmalloc0), meaning the MAC label/profile is left to whatever the exec engine defaults to rather than being inherited from the container's config here.

Only when the user passed one or more `--cap` options (cap_size > 0) does crun build a capabilities struct. It sets ALL five capability sets — effective, inheritable, bounding, ambient, permitted — to the SAME user-supplied list exec_options.cap (effective aliases the option array; the other four are dup_array copies). Critically, if cap_size == 0 this whole block is skipped and process->capabilities remains NULL, so the process spec expresses NO capability policy at all for the exec'd process.

Sets the no_new_privileges bit ONLY if the user passed `--no-new-privs`; otherwise the field keeps its zero (false) value from xmalloc0. The in-code comment (untrusted) rationalizes this. Finally the assembled process spec is attached to exec_opts.process for the exec call. The default here decides whether the new process can gain privileges via setuid/setgid binaries or file capabilities after exec.

what_the_code_does.code:
exec_opts.cgroup = exec_options.cgroup;

  return libcrun_container_exec_with_options (&crun_context, argv[first_arg], &exec_opts, err);
what_the_code_does.explanation: Records the target cgroup and calls libcrun_container_exec_with_options with the runtime context, the container id, the fully-assembled exec_opts (which carries either the spec-file path or the synthesized process struct), and the error out-param. This is where crun actually joins the container namespaces and executes the process under whatever privilege policy the fields above encoded.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the core CWE-276 (Incorrect Default Permissions) defect. process was zero-allocated (line 39), so process->no_new_privileges is 0/false by default, and it is set to 1 ONLY when the operator explicitly passes `--no-new-privs`. That means `crun exec` launches the new process WITHOUT the no_new_privileges bit even when the original container was created with no_new_privileges = true. Inside the container, a setuid/setgid binary or a binary with file capabilities can then raise the effective privilege of the exec'd process, defeating a confinement guarantee the container owner reasonably expects to persist across exec. The invariant the implementation must uphold: `crun exec` must inherit/default no_new_privileges from the container's stored config (basespec), not silently reset it to false. This matches CVE-2022-27650 in crun.

Two distinct hazards live here, both CWE-276. (1) When cap_size == 0 the block is skipped and process->capabilities stays NULL, so the exec'd process gets no explicit capability policy from this path — depending on the exec engine, the new process may retain the caller's/host capabilities rather than being confined to the container's intended (typically reduced) capability set. The safe invariant is that exec must default the capability sets from the container's stored config, not leave them unset. (2) When cap_size > 0, crun copies the SAME user list into ALL FIVE sets, including `inheritable` and `ambient`. Populating inheritable/ambient capabilities is precisely how privileges can be carried across an exec into a child binary; equating inheritable/ambient with the requested effective/permitted set is an over-grant. This over-broad inheritable/ambient assignment is the sibling defect to CVE-2022-27649/27650 (containers started with non-empty inheritable capabilities).

args_len is set to `argc`, but the array is only populated with `argc - first_arg` real entries (indices 0 .. argc-first_arg-1) plus a NULL terminator at index argc-first_arg. Because first_arg is normally >= 1 (the container id precedes the command), args_len OVER-COUNTS the real arguments. Any downstream consumer that trusts args_len as the number of valid argv entries will iterate past the NULL terminator into the zero-filled tail of the allocation and dereference NULL char* slots (CWE-476) or emit bogus/empty arguments. The allocation itself is (argc+1) slots so there is no out-of-bounds write on the array, but the length/content mismatch is a real correctness-and-safety contract violation: args_len should equal the number of tokens actually copied (argc - first_arg), and the array should be sized to that count.

The LISTEN_FDS environment variable is parsed with strtoll and no validation: a non-numeric value yields 0 (benign), but a negative value (e.g. "-1") or an absurdly large value is accepted verbatim and ADDED to preserve_fds. Depending on the width/signedness of preserve_fds and how the exec engine loops over `0..preserve_fds` to keep/close FDs, a negative or huge count can cause it to attempt to preserve file descriptors that were never opened (leaking host FDs into the container) or to iterate over an enormous range. The invariant: LISTEN_FDS must be range-checked (>= 0 and within a sane FD ceiling) and its string fully validated before being folded into preserve_fds. CWE-20 (improper input validation); potential CWE-190 if the addition overflows the counter type.

When `--process` is supplied, the entire else-branch (lines 38-89) that would default capabilities and no_new_privileges is skipped and crun trusts the spec file wholesale. This is defensible IF the spec file is authored by the same trust domain, but it means there are two divergent code paths for building the exec identity, and only the file path avoids the insecure defaults above. Worth confirming that the file-based path enforces the same confinement floor (no_new_privileges, dropped capabilities) as the container's original config, rather than accepting an under-restricted process spec.

summary: This is crun's `crun exec` handler. Its central defect is CWE-276 Incorrect Default Permissions: no_new_privileges is only set when `--no-new-privs` is passed (defaulting to false), and capabilities are only configured when `--cap` is passed, so an exec'd process can silently run with weaker confinement than the container was created with — matching CVE-2022-27650. When capabilities ARE set they are over-broadly copied into all five sets (including inheritable/ambient). Secondary issues: process->args_len is set to argc while only argc-first_arg args are populated (length/content mismatch, possible NULL deref downstream), and LISTEN_FDS is folded into preserve_fds without range/error validation.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: crun_command_exec(struct crun_global_arguments*, int argc, char **argv, libcrun_error_t*)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry-point signature; carries no direct memory/privilege contract itself — the contracts live in the fields it assembles below.
Zero-initialized context aggregate; individual security-relevant members (preserve_fds/listen_fds) are reviewed separately.
ABI/version guard stamped from sizeof; benign and correct.
preserve_fds is derived from environment-controlled LISTEN_FDS without validation.
Standard argp CLI parse; argv is user-supplied but the resulting options are individually handled/validated.
Arity guard that bounds first_arg indexing; acts as a safety check, no negative contract.
argv[first_arg] is in range after the arity check; return value is checked.
Environment value parsed with no range/error validation and added to preserve_fds.
Spec-file branch bypasses the default capability/no_new_privileges logic; divergent trust path worth confirming.
Set to argc while only argc-first_arg args are populated — length/content mismatch.
Part of the args_len mismatch; array content/length disagree, tied to the finding above.
Direct option copies; env_len tracks the caller-owned env array size, no fixed-buffer contract here.

Set only when explicitly provided; leaving them NULL defers to engine defaults rather than creating a distinct overflow contract (subsumed by the general MAC/default posture noted in capabilities finding).
cap_size==0 leaves capabilities NULL (incorrect default), and cap_size>0 over-copies the same list into inheritable/ambient (over-grant).
Defaults to false unless --no-new-privs passed — the primary CWE-276 defect (CVE-2022-27650).
Plain option pass-through; no size or privilege contract in this file.
Consumer of exec_opts; the dangerous defaults it acts on are attributed to the fields above.

findings.title: crun exec defaults no_new_privileges to false (privilege escalation across exec)
findings.bug_class: Incorrect default permissions (missing security bit on exec)
findings.invariant: `crun exec` must launch the new process with no_new_privileges at least as restrictive as the container's stored config (basespec); the flag must be inherited/defaulted from the container, never silently reset to false when the operator omits --no-new-privs.
findings.failure_mode: process is xmalloc0'd so no_new_privileges is false by default and only set to 1 when --no-new-privs is passed. A user who execs into a container created with no_new_privileges=true gets a process WITHOUT the bit, so a setuid/setgid binary or a binary with file capabilities inside the container can raise the process's privileges, breaking the container's intended confinement and enabling privilege escalation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether libcrun_container_exec_with_options re-derives no_new_privileges from the container's stored config; if it trusts process->no_new_privileges as authoritative, the false default is directly exploitable.

findings.title: Capabilities left NULL when no --cap given, and inheritable/ambient over-granted when set
findings.bug_class: Incorrect default permissions / over-broad capability inheritance
findings.invariant: When no --cap is supplied, the exec'd process must default to the container's stored (reduced) capability sets, not to an unset/NULL policy; when caps ARE supplied, inheritable and ambient sets must be assigned conservatively (typically NOT equal to the full requested effective/permitted set) so privileges are not carried across the subsequent exec.
findings.failure_mode: If cap_size==0 the block is skipped and process->capabilities stays NULL, so the new process may retain the caller's/host capabilities instead of the container's intended reduced set. If cap_size>0, the same user list is copied into all five sets including inheritable and ambient, which are exactly the sets that propagate privileges to a child binary — an over-grant that can hand elevated capabilities to the exec'd program.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the exec engine's behavior when process->capabilities is NULL (does it inherit container config or host caps?), and whether populating inheritable/ambient equal to effective is intended or should be empty by default.

findings.title: process->args_len set to argc but only argc-first_arg args populated
findings.anchor: process->args_len = argc; ... for (i = 0; i < argc - first_arg; i++) process->args[i] = xstrdup(...)
findings.bug_class: Length/content mismatch leading to NULL-pointer read downstream
findings.invariant: args_len must equal the number of argument strings actually written into process->args (argc - first_arg); consumers must never read more than that many valid entries.
findings.failure_mode: args_len is argc but only argc-first_arg entries are filled (the rest, up to index argc, are zeroed by xmalloc0 including the explicit NULL terminator). Since first_arg is normally >= 1, any consumer that iterates args_len entries walks past the NULL terminator and dereferences NULL char* slots (crash / undefined behavior) or serializes empty/garbage arguments into the OCI spec.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify how process->args_len is consumed by the JSON/OCI serializer and the exec path; confirm whether it should be argc - first_arg.

findings.title: LISTEN_FDS environment value folded into preserve_fds without validation
findings.bug_class: Improper input validation of environment-controlled counter
findings.invariant: LISTEN_FDS must be parsed with error detection and range-checked (non-negative and within a sane FD ceiling) before being added to preserve_fds; the addition must not overflow the counter type.
findings.failure_mode: A negative LISTEN_FDS (e.g. "-1") or an enormous value is accepted verbatim and added to preserve_fds. Depending on how the exec engine loops over the preserve_fds range to keep/close descriptors, this can leak host file descriptors into the container or drive an oversized iteration; a huge value can also overflow the counter (CWE-190).
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the type/width of preserve_fds and how it bounds the FD-preservation loop in the exec engine; add range/error checks on the strtoll result.

audit_checklist: Does libcrun_container_exec_with_options default no_new_privileges from the container's stored config when process->no_new_privileges is 0, or does it treat the false default as authoritative (CVE-2022-27650)?
When process->capabilities is NULL (no --cap), does the exec engine confine the process to the container's reduced capability set, or does it inherit the caller's/host capabilities?
Should inheritable and ambient capability sets default to empty rather than being copied equal to the requested effective/permitted set (lines 68-75)?
Confirm process->args_len should be argc - first_arg, and audit every consumer of args_len for reads past the NULL terminator (lines 42-46).
Add error/range validation for LISTEN_FDS (non-numeric, negative, overflow) before adding it to preserve_fds (lines 31-32).
Verify the --process spec-file path (line 36) enforces the same no_new_privileges/capability confinement floor as the synthesized path.
